planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; jackfruit is frost-sensitive and suited only to zones 10–12, though established trees briefly tolerate temperatures down to about 28–30°F. Trees grow rapidly once established and require substantial spacing to accommodate their large canopy and the world's largest tree-borne fruit, which can weigh up to 80 pounds.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zones 10–12; jackfruit (Artocarpus heterophyllus) is a fast-growing tropical tree from India and Bangladesh that produces the world's largest tree-borne fruit — massive, spiky green-to-yellow pods weighing 10–80 pounds that hang directly from the trunk and main branches in a spectacular display of cauliflory. The flesh (arils) surrounding each seed is sweet, fragrant, and fiber-rich, eaten fresh when ripe or cooked as a meat substitute when unripe — a use that has made jackfruit globally popular as a plant-based protein source. Trees grafted from superior selections begin fruiting in 3–4 years and can produce 100–200 fruits per year on mature specimens. Plant in well-drained soil, as jackfruit is highly susceptible to root rot in waterlogged conditions; once established, trees are moderately drought-tolerant and grow vigorously in the humid tropics.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System
Morton, J. Fruits of Warm Climates

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; breadfruit is one of the most productive food plants in the tropics and is propagated from root cuttings or air layers rather than seed, as most cultivated varieties are seedless. Give each tree ample space — at least 25–40 feet — as the canopy spreads broadly and the tree grows rapidly to 40–85 feet tall.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zones 10–12; breadfruit (Artocarpus altilis) is a Polynesian staple food tree that has been cultivated throughout the Pacific and Caribbean for centuries, prized for its large, starchy fruit that can be roasted, boiled, fried, or dried into flour — providing a remarkably complete carbohydrate staple that sustained entire island civilizations. A single mature tree can produce 50–200 or more large fruits per year, yielding hundreds of pounds of nutritious food with minimal inputs. Most improved varieties are seedless triploids propagated from root suckers or cuttings; plant these for the fastest fruiting and best productivity. Trees are highly sensitive to frost and cold winds, but once established in a frost-free tropical location they are vigorous, long-lived, and capable of producing fruit within 3–5 years of planting.
sources: Breadfruit Institute, National Tropical Botanical Garden
USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained, limestone-tolerant soil in full sun; Maya nut is a large canopy tree of Mesoamerican tropical forests that grows in zones 10–12 and can tolerate brief cold to about 32°F in protected zone 9b microclimates. The seeds must be planted fresh, as viability drops rapidly; sow immediately after extraction from ripe fruit.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil — including rocky, limestone-based soils — in full sun in zones 10–12; Maya nut (Brosimum alicastrum) is a towering canopy tree in the Moraceae family that the ancient Maya and other Mesoamerican peoples relied upon as a critical famine food for thousands of years, with seeds that are extraordinarily nutritious — high in protein, calcium, potassium, iron, zinc, and B vitamins — and can be boiled, roasted, dried, and ground into flour. The ripe purple-skinned fruits are also edible fresh, with a sweet, fig-like flavor, and the abundant leaves serve as high-quality livestock fodder. Trees are long-lived, moderately drought-tolerant once established, and grow naturally in limestone-rich soils where other fruit trees may struggle. Though primarily a large timber and food tree rather than an orchard crop, Maya nut is gaining recognition as an exceptionally valuable multipurpose agroforestry species, with first seed harvests typically beginning 5–10 years after planting.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; abiu is a South American tropical tree suited to zones 10–12 that performs best in consistently warm, humid conditions with ample rainfall or irrigation. Grafted trees from superior selections begin fruiting in 3–4 years and produce smooth, bright yellow fruits with translucent, very sweet, jelly-like flesh.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun in zones 10–12; abiu (Pouteria caimito) is a tropical Sapotaceae tree native to the Amazon basin that produces smooth, glossy, bright lemon-yellow fruits with a custardy, translucent white flesh of exceptional sweetness — often described as tasting of caramel, vanilla, or crème brûlée — that clings to a small number of large seeds. The fruit is eaten fresh by scooping out the flesh, taking care to avoid the slightly sticky latex in the skin, which can irritate the lips if left in contact. Abiu is widely grown in Brazil and Queensland, Australia, where its productivity and flavor have made it one of the most popular exotic Sapotaceae fruits among tropical fruit enthusiasts. Trees respond well to regular fertilization with a balanced tropical fruit fertilizer and benefit from consistent moisture during fruit development; mulch heavily to conserve soil moisture and maintain root health.
sources: Queensland Department of Agriculture and Fisheries
Morton, J. Fruits of Warm Climates

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; mamey sapote is a Central American tropical tree suited to zones 10–12, with mature established trees tolerating brief drops to 28–30°F in protected zone 9b microclimates. Named grafted varieties such as 'Magaña' and 'Pantin' begin producing the distinctive brown-skinned, salmon-fleshed fruits within 3–5 years of planting.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zones 10–12; mamey sapote (Pouteria sapota) is a majestic Central American tree in the Sapotaceae family that produces one of the most beloved tropical fruits in Caribbean and Latin American cuisine — a large, russet-brown, football-shaped fruit with vivid salmon-to-brick-red flesh of exceptional richness, sweetness, and smooth texture, often compared to a pumpkin-sweet potato-almond fusion. The fruit is eaten fresh, blended into milkshakes and batidos (a staple across Miami's Cuban community and throughout the Caribbean), made into ice cream, and used in desserts. Only grafted trees of named selections should be planted, as seedling trees can take 10–15 years to fruit and produce variable quality; grafted trees begin bearing in 3–5 years. Mamey sapote is moderately drought-tolerant once established but produces larger, higher-quality fruit with consistent irrigation and regular fertilization with a balanced citrus-type fertilizer.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System
Morton, J. Fruits of Warm Climates

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sapodilla is one of the most drought- and salt-tolerant tropical fruit trees available, making it well-suited to South Florida's sandy, alkaline soils and coastal conditions. Mature established trees tolerate brief cold to 26–28°F, which allows planting in protected zone 9b microclimates.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil — including sandy, rocky, and calcareous soils — in full sun in zones 10–12; sapodilla (Manilkara zapota) is a long-lived, slow-growing Mesoamerican tree that ranks among the most drought- and wind-tolerant of all tropical fruit trees, making it exceptionally well adapted to South Florida's coastal sandy soils, salt air, and seasonal dry periods. The small to medium-sized, rough brown-skinned fruits reveal a translucent, grainy, caramel-brown flesh of extraordinary sweetness — often described as tasting of brown sugar, pear, and malt — that must be fully ripe and soft before eating, as unripe fruits are astringent from tannins. Grafted trees of selections such as 'Prolific' and 'Tikal' begin bearing in 4–6 years; seedling trees may take 8 years or more. The tree also has historical industrial significance as the source of chicle, the natural latex once used as the base of chewing gum, harvested by the Mesoamerican chiclero tradition for centuries.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System
Morton, J. Fruits of Warm Climates
